sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A psychology department ran a randomized trial of a 10-minute daily mindfulness app during finals season. One hundred twenty students were randomly assigned either to the mindfulness app or to a daily weather-journal control task. Stress was measured on a 1–10 scale at baseline and after 14 days. Mean stress in the mindfulness group changed from 7.4 to 5.8, while the control group changed from 7.3 to 6.9. Average nightly sleep increased by 0.6 hours in the mindfulness group and 0.1 hours in the control group. App logs showed that not every student completed every daily session.
questions.stem: Which group showed the larger reduction in stress over 14 days?

explanations.D: Correct. The mindfulness group fell from 7.4 to 5.8, a 1.6-point drop. The control group fell only from 7.3 to 6.9, a 0.4-point drop, so the larger reduction is clearly in the mindfulness-app group.
explanations.A: The control group improved less than the mindfulness group.
explanations.B: The groups changed by different amounts, not equally.
explanations.C: Both groups changed, especially the mindfulness group.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean baseline and day-14 stress scores in the mindfulness-app and control groups.

questions.stem: Which design feature most strengthens a causal interpretation of the intervention effect?

choices.A: Random assignment to mindfulness versus control
choices.D: Measuring sleep as an additional variable

explanations.A: Correct. Random assignment makes the two groups more comparable at baseline and helps isolate the intervention as the main systematic difference. That is the key feature supporting a causal interpretation of the stress reduction.
explanations.B: The scale format alone does not establish causality.
explanations.C: App logs help monitor adherence but do not by themselves create causal inference.
explanations.D: Additional variables can enrich the study, but they are not the main reason causal inference is stronger.

questions.stem: The fact that not every student completed every session primarily threatens what aspect of the study?

choices.A: Construct validity of the stress scale
choices.B: Population validity because the sample was students

explanations.C: Correct. If participants do not consistently use the intervention, then the delivered treatment differs from the intended treatment. That is primarily an issue of adherence or intervention fidelity.
explanations.A: The passage does not suggest that the stress scale fails to measure stress.
explanations.B: Using students does affect generalizability, but incomplete session use is mainly about treatment delivery.
explanations.D: The control task was clearly specified; the issue is whether participants completed their assigned activity.

questions.stem: Which result would be most consistent with mindfulness improving stress partly through better sleep?

choices.B: Students with the largest sleep gains within the mindfulness group also showed the largest stress reductions
choices.A: Control participants who disliked weather journaling had higher stress at baseline
choices.C: Mindfulness participants differed in major field of study
choices.D: Students in both groups completed exams during the same week

explanations.B: Correct. If sleep is part of the pathway from the intervention to lower stress, then improvements in sleep should covary with stress reduction among those receiving the app. That pattern would support mediation by sleep.
explanations.A: Baseline dislike of journaling does not specifically test sleep as a mechanism.
explanations.C: Academic major is not directly relevant to the proposed sleep pathway.
explanations.D: Taking exams during the same week standardizes context but does not test mediation.

choices.A: The mindfulness app was associated with a larger average stress reduction than the control task in this randomized sample
choices.B: Mindfulness permanently eliminates stress in college students
choices.C: Weather journaling increases stress in all users
choices.D: The study proves sleep is the only mechanism involved

explanations.A: Correct. The randomized comparison supports the conclusion that the app group improved more on average over the 14-day period. The results do not justify extreme claims about permanence, universality, or a single mechanism.
explanations.B: The data show improvement over 14 days, not permanent elimination of stress.
explanations.C: The control group also improved slightly, so journaling did not clearly increase stress.
explanations.D: Sleep may contribute, but the study does not show it is the only mechanism.

questions.passage: A survey methods course demonstrated wording effects using two versions of a campus safety question. Half of respondents were asked whether 'serious violence is a major problem on campus,' while the other half were asked whether 'safety incidents occasionally occur on campus.' In the first wording condition, 62% agreed; in the second, 34% agreed. A third item asked whether students had personally changed their behavior because of safety concerns; 19% said yes. Follow-up interviews indicated that some respondents interpreted 'serious violence' broadly, while others interpreted 'safety incidents' as including minor issues like poor lighting.
questions.stem: What does Figure 2 most clearly illustrate?

choices.D: Response rates changed substantially depending on how the question was worded
choices.A: Students never worry about campus safety
choices.B: Behavior change was more common than agreement with either wording
choices.C: The two wording conditions produced nearly identical responses

explanations.D: Correct. Agreement was 62% for the “serious violence” wording and 34% for the “safety incidents” wording. That large difference shows how wording can influence measured attitudes or perceptions.
explanations.A: Nineteen percent reported behavior change, so some students did worry about safety.
explanations.B: Behavior change was less common than agreement with either wording condition.
explanations.C: The two wording conditions differ by 28 percentage points, so they are far from identical.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Agreement percentages for two differently worded safety items and a behavior-change item.

questions.stem: The wording effect is a threat to which type of validity?

explanations.C: Correct. If different phrasings produce different interpretations of what is being asked, then the survey item may not consistently capture the intended construct. That is a concern about construct validity.
explanations.A: Ecological validity concerns realism of the setting, not whether the item measures the intended concept.
explanations.B: Statistical power concerns sample size and precision, not wording interpretation.
explanations.D: Face recognition memory is unrelated to survey validity.

questions.stem: Why might the “serious violence” wording have produced higher agreement?

choices.B: The emotionally loaded phrase may have cued respondents to think about alarming events
choices.C: Because respondents cannot understand the word “violence”
choices.D: Because it prevented any interpretation variability

explanations.B: Correct. Loaded wording can activate vivid examples and a stronger emotional response, increasing endorsement. The follow-up interviews suggest that respondents did not all interpret the terms identically, which supports this explanation.
explanations.A: Length alone is not the most plausible mechanism here.
explanations.C: The passage does not suggest incomprehension of the word “violence.”
explanations.D: The interviews indicate the opposite: interpretations varied considerably.

questions.stem: A researcher who wants a more accurate estimate of campus safety perceptions should most likely do what?

choices.D: Pilot-test neutral wording and define key terms clearly
choices.A: Use the most alarming language possible to maximize concern
choices.B: Ask only one broad item and avoid follow-up questions
choices.C: Treat the first wording as automatically correct because it produced more agreement

explanations.D: Correct. Pilot testing and clarification can reduce ambiguity and help ensure that respondents are answering the same question. That is the most direct way to improve measurement quality given the wording effects shown.
explanations.A: Alarming language may distort responses rather than improve accuracy.
explanations.B: One broad ambiguous item would preserve rather than solve the measurement problem.
explanations.C: Higher agreement does not mean the item is more accurate.

questions.stem: Which interpretation of the 19% behavior-change item is most defensible?

choices.A: It reflects a different outcome from the wording experiment and cannot be directly equated with perceived campus danger
choices.B: It proves that 81% of students feel perfectly safe
choices.C: It shows the wording manipulation had no effect on any measure
choices.D: It should be averaged with the other two percentages to estimate “true” safety concern

explanations.A: Correct. Changing behavior because of safety concerns is a behavioral report, not simply an attitude judgment. It may be related to perceived danger, but it measures a distinct construct and should not be treated as interchangeable with agreement to the wording conditions.
explanations.B: Not changing behavior does not prove that a student feels perfectly safe.
explanations.C: The wording manipulation clearly affected the two attitudinal items.
explanations.D: Averaging unlike measures would mix different constructs rather than reveal a true underlying value.
